channel.title: Hello, Body
channel.description: Plain-language writing about women's health from Heather Cole, a nurse practitioner in Green Bay, Wisconsin. New essays every other Sunday.
channel.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP
channel.itunes:summary: A small newsletter and audio reading on women's health. Endometriosis, contraception, the slow work of getting a diagnosis taken seriously.

item.title: How to talk to a new clinician about pain you have had for years
item.description: A short script for the appointment where you finally say it all out loud, and what to do if it still does not land.
item.content:encoded:
The first visit is rarely the one where everything gets solved. It is the visit where you set the stage. Bring a one-page summary, name the symptom, name how it affects your week, and name what you have already tried. Ask one direct question.

If the clinician does not engage, that is data. You are allowed to leave and book somewhere else.
item.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP

item.title: Managing flare weeks without quitting your life
item.description: A weekly plan I built with patients who flare every month. Heat, movement, and a permission slip to slow down.
item.content:encoded: The week of a flare is not a week to set goals. It is a week to set a floor. Heat in the morning. One walk if you can. Protein at lunch. Lights off by ten. The smallest version of a normal week is still a week.
item.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP

item.title: The blood test that is almost never useful, and the one that often is
item.description: CA-125 is not a screening test. Ferritin is underused. A short post for anyone tired of being told the labs are normal.
item.content:encoded: If you have pelvic pain, you are likely to be told the labs look fine. That sentence is not the end of the conversation. It is the start.
item.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP

item.title: Year one as an NP, in five honest paragraphs
item.description: What the first twelve months actually felt like. Charting, the inbox, and the patient who taught me to ask the second question.
item.content:encoded: The first year is mostly notes. Notes you take home. Notes you wake up at four in the morning to fix. The clinic gets easier when you stop trying to write the perfect note and start writing the useful one.
item.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP

item.title: A short reading list for new women's health NPs
item.description: Six titles that taught me more than any course. Not a complete list. A useful one.
item.content:encoded: The books that helped were the ones I returned to between visits. ACOG bulletins, one solid endometriosis textbook, and a slim book on motivational interviewing.
item.itunes:author: Heather Cole, APRN-NP
